headline: What if financial literacy could boost GDP?
summary: New research suggests a direct link between improved understanding of issues such as inflation and lower loan defaults
body_text:
The writer is the FT’s deputy editor, and chair of the FT’s Financial Literacy and Inclusion Campaign, which supports financial literacy programmes in the UK and around the world

Back in the spring Isabel Schnabel, a respected executive board member at the European Central Bank, added her voice to the growing chorus of support for financial literacy education — not out of any do-gooding sense of beneficence, but because, she said, monetary policy works better when citizens understand the basics of finance.

“Financially literate individuals react more strongly to interest rate changes, are more willing to take on risk and are more forward-looking when forming inflation expectations,” she told Bayes Business School in her Mais Lecture.

In other words, monetary transmission is more effective if society knows what the inflation rate is, and how interest rates might rise or fall over time, because that knowledge will make you more likely to borrow, spend and invest wisely, rather than ra

headline: Federal Reserve preview: rates to fall on Wednesday but December’s cut less certain
summary: The Fed will move lower this week despite lack of new data due to the government shutdown
body_text:
All the pieces are in place for the Fed to cut rates again when it meets on Wednesday. Having pushed ahead with its September cut and revealed new official forecasts showing median expectations for a total of three cuts in 2025, the move is widely expected.

Had chair Powell wanted to push back, his speech at the IMF on October 14 would have been the time, instead he said that “the downside risks to employment appear to have risen”.

Powell dedicated his speech then to the balance sheet and suggested that quantitative tightening could end “in the coming months”.

With another December cut fully priced in by the futures market, we will be looking for any hints of forward guidance. Given the lack of official data due to the government shutdown, this could be limited.

The Fed story has evolved only slightly since rates were cut in September to 4 to 4.25 per cent. With the US government closed for business since the beginning of October, official data on jobs remains absent. It is still s
image_caption: Fed chair Jay Powell may address the issue of quantitative tightening on Wednesday

headline: RIP QT
body_text:
Meta and Microsoft had mixed earnings reports, the Federal Reserve cut interest rates and said it would halt quantitative tightening and South Korea has agreed to invest $350bn in the US in return for lower tariffs on car exports. Plus, John Malone is stepping down as chair of his media and telecoms empire, marking the end of an era in which the “cable cowboy” reshaped both industries.

Mentioned in this podcast:

Meta hit by huge AI spending

Federal Reserve trims US interest rates by quarter point but casts doubt on December cut

Federal Reserve nears end of QT amid signs of stress in money markets

US and South Korea seal trade deal

‘Cable cowboy’ John Malone to step down from media and telecoms empire

Today’s FT News Briefing was produced by Lucy Baldwin, Sonja Hutson, and Marc Filippino. Our show was mixed by Kelly Garry. Additional help from Gavin Kallmann and Michael Lello. The FT’s acting co-head of audio is Topher Forhecz. The show’s theme music is by Metaphor Music.

headline: Transcript: RIP QT
summary: Marc Filippino talks to Claire Jones and Kieran Smith
body_text:
This is an audio transcript of the FT News Briefing podcast episode: ‘RIP QT’

Marc FilippinoGood morning from the Financial Times. Today is Thursday, October 30th, and this is your FT News Briefing. Meta had tech earnings yesterday it probably wants to forget, Microsoft, not so much. And the Federal Reserve is gonna stop shrinking its balance sheet. Plus, the cable cowboy is saying howdy to retirement. I’m Marc Filippino, and here’s the news you need to start your day.

[MUSIC PLAYING]

Meta and Microsoft left investors with mixed feelings when they reported earnings yesterday. Let’s start with Meta. Revenues rose 26 per cent last quarter, but its net income fell by a whopping 83 per cent. The company said that was because of a one-off income tax charge of nearly $15bn. That charge is related to US President Donald Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill. Meta’s share price dropped more than 7 per cent in after-hours trading. Then there was Microsoft, and this one really comes down to investor

School IB economics: US adds 119,000 jobs in September but unemployment hits four-year peak
body_text:
This article picked by a teacher with suggested questions is part of the Financial Times free schools access programme. Details/registration here.

Specification:

Some current statistics about US monetary policy and unemployment:

The current rate of unemployment in the US is 4.4 per cent, the third lowest in the G7 behind Japan (2.56 per cent) and Germany (3.41 per cent)

The US inflation rate of 3 per cent is close to its target rate, but the second-highest in the G7.

Unemployment is measured by the US Bureau of Labor Statistics

US President Donald Trump has long campaigned for the Fed to cut rates, arguing that high interest rates act as a barrier to jobs and investment. But the Federal Reserve is independent and must consider a range of factors when deciding to cut rates,

Read the article and then answer the questions:

US adds 119,000 jobs in September but unemployment hits four-year peak

Mark Johnson, InThinking/thinkIB

Gold, silver and crypto
summary: What’s driving the run-up in alternatives to fiat currency?
body_text:
Silver is on a tear this year, outperforming gold and even crypto. Today on the show, Rob Armstrong and Katie Martin look at the markets for substitutes for fiat currency. Also they go long the Christmas goose, and long sweet pastry.

For a free 30-day trial to the Unhedged newsletter go to: https://www.ft.com/unhedgedoffer.

You can email Robert Armstrong and Katie Martin at unhedged@ft.com.

Read a transcript of this episode on FT.com

headline: Transcript: Gold, silver and crypto
summary: Katie Martin and Robert Armstrong discuss what’s driving the run-up in substitutes to fiat currency
body_text:
This is an audio transcript of the Unhedged podcast episode: ‘Gold, silver and crypto’

[MUSIC PLAYING]

Katie MartinThis has been a big year for shiny stuff. Gold, of course, has had a spectacular run higher, cruising to $4,000 an ounce and beyond. But stand aside, gold, because your scrappy little cousin, silver, would like a word. What a year it has been for silver. So gold’s up like 60-ish per cent, silver’s up a 120 per cent this year.

[MUSIC PLAYING]

Silver fever, boys and girls get on board. So is the rise in the price of silver a sign of fear in markets or a sign that bubbly exuberance is taking hold. And what does this all have to do with gold? And also, I hate to say it, but with bitcoin. Today on the show, fear, greed and slightly wacky paranoid world views.

This is Unhedged, the markets and finance podcast from the Financial Times and Pushkin. I’m Katie Martin, a markets economist here at the FT and very Christmassy London. Have some hard yards of Christmas drinks ahead

headline: JPMorgan swaps cash for Treasuries
summary: JPMorgan's withdrawals from Federal Reserve to buy Treasuries total $350bn
body_text:
Investors are snapping up Venezuela’s defaulted debt, JPMorgan Chase has withdrawn almost $350bn in cash from its account at the Federal Reserve since 2023, and the US unemployment rate rose to its highest level in more than four years. Plus, global asset managers’ cash holdings have fallen to a record low in a sign of investors’ bullishness about the AI-fuelled stock market rally.

Mentioned in this podcast:

Investors pile into Venezuelan debt in regime change bet

JPMorgan pulls $350bn from Federal Reserve to buy up Treasuries

Bullish investors pile into stocks as cash levels sink to record low

US unemployment rate hits four-year high of 4.6%

Fifa offers cheaper World Cup tickets in response to outcry

Note: The FT does not use generative AI to voice its podcasts

Today’s FT News Briefing was hosted and edited by Marc Filippino, and produced by Victoria Craig and Sonja Hutson. Our show was mixed by Kelly Garry. Additional help from Gavin Kallmann. The FT’s acting co-head of audio

headline: Transcript: Three numbers that matter
summary: Katie Martin and Rob Armstrong discuss new figures about jobs, inflation and leverage
body_text:
This is an audio transcript of the Unhedged podcast episode: ‘Three numbers that matter’

Katie MartinHow healthy is the US economy, really? It’s a tricky question to answer in all honesty, because the economic data is still a bit screwy since the federal government shut down earlier this year.

If you are confused, don’t worry, because Donald Trump says it’s great.

[MUSIC PLAYING]

We are poised for an economic boom, the likes of which the world has never seen, he said this week. Poised, maybe. The general public, though, is not convinced. People are worried about all sorts of things — inflation and that kind of thing and they’re cautious on spending.

Today on the show: inflation, jobs and market plumbing, we’ll tell you the numbers that matter.

[MUSIC PLAYING]

This is Unhedged, the markets and finance podcasts from the Financial Times and Pushkin.

I’m Katie Martin — a slightly hungover markets columnist with coffee spill on my white top — here at FT towers in London. It’s one of

headline: Martin Wolf speaks to Christine Lagarde: Europe’s ‘existential crisis’
summary: The economy has been surprisingly resilient but there is much work to do, says the ECB president
body_text:
The European Commission must urgently dismantle internal trade barriers that are stifling innovation, productivity and investment across the EU. So says Christine Lagarde, president of the European Central Bank, in a conversation with the FT’s chief economics commentator, Martin Wolf, as she calls for immediate structural reforms and explains why this is the moment to do it.

This interview was recorded on December 10 as part of the FT’s The Global Boardroom conference.

Subscribe and listen to this series of The Economics Show on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Pocket Casts or wherever you listen to podcasts.

Read Martin’s column here

Sound design and original music by Breen Turner.

Read a transcript of this episode on FT.com

headline: Inflation and interest rates tracker: see how your country compares
summary: Inflationary pressures are beginning to wane. Explore the latest data through interactive charts
body_text:
The FT global inflation and interest rates tracker provides a regularly updated picture of consumer price inflation and central bank policy rates around the world.

Our tracker features the latest inflation and policy rate data for most countries, historical figures, as well as key factors affecting policymakers’ decisions on borrowing costs. Bookmark this page to keep up to date as the global inflation story evolves.

Many central banks have been lowering borrowing costs as global inflation gradually eases from the multi-decade highs recently recorded in many countries.

While inflation in most nations has come down from its peak, many policymakers have warned that the last leg of the journey to central banks’ target — which in most advanced economies is 2 per cent — will be the hardest.

This chart shows the outcome of central bank decisions since the pandemic, when many policymakers moved to lower borrowing costs.

headline: Inflation and interest rates tracker: see how your country compares
summary: Inflationary pressures are beginning to wane. Explore the latest data through interactive charts
body_text:
The FT global inflation and interest rates tracker provides a regularly updated picture of consumer price inflation and central bank policy rates around the world.

Our tracker features the latest inflation and policy rate data for most countries, historical figures, as well as key factors affecting policymakers’ decisions on borrowing costs. Bookmark this page to keep up to date as the global inflation story evolves.

Many central banks have been lowering borrowing costs as global inflation gradually eases from the multi-decade highs recently recorded in many countries.

While inflation in most nations has come down from its peak, many policymakers have warned that the last leg of the journey to central banks’ target — which in most advanced economies is 2 per cent — will be the hardest.
